original_reasoning: Arabic: السقلب (a people/ethnic group, Slavs), verb meaning to throw down. Greek: Slav. This is a direct loanword — both refer to the Slavic peoples. The Arabic al-Saqlab is itself borrowed from Greek/Latin Sclavus. Near-identity.

Arabic فرخ = chick/nestling/young of a bird. Greek φάρκες = young birds/nestlings/chicks (Hesychius: νεοσσοί). Direct semantic match — both mean exactly 'young birds/nestlings'. Strong cognate candidate.
Arabic says one is struck senseless or faints; Greek directly means be faint.
Both sides are proper-name forms with very close phonetic preservation, making this an almost direct name match.
Henna is a cultivated plant, and λάχανον is a garden herb; the plant/cultivated-herb core is near-identical.
This is near-identical as a transmitted place-name form: consonants and referential identity are both preserved closely.
